meta.first_prompt: Goldy is a set of claude skills and agenst that helps developer keep in touch with what is being done to their codebase when using claude code. Goldy does the following: 1. Exports the whole thinking and implementation process for it to be…
meta.title: Building Goldy
meta.summary: Goldy is a set of Claude Code skills and agents that turn a session into a readable report. This walkthrough traces how it was built, step by step, with the reasoning and sources behind each decision.

nodes.rationale: The brief is three coupled requirements: capture the full reasoning and action trace, attach a justification and learning links to each step, and present it as a polished Notion-like document. That framing drove the parse, enrich, render split that follows.

materials.title: Extend Claude with Skills, Claude Code Docs
materials.note: The SKILL.md frontmatter and progressive-disclosure model Goldy's skill is built on.
materials.summary: A skill is a folder containing a SKILL.md file whose YAML frontmatter (name and description) is preloaded into the system prompt so Claude knows when to use it. The fuller instructions, plus optional scripts, references and assets, load only when the skill is actually triggered. This progressive disclosure keeps base context small while still giving the agent deep, on-demand capability.

materials.title: Create custom subagents, Claude Code Docs
materials.note: Defines the ~/.claude/agents/*.md format and tool and model scoping used by goldy-historian.
materials.summary: Custom subagents are Markdown files with YAML frontmatter stored in .claude/agents (project) or ~/.claude/agents (user). Each subagent gets its own system prompt, its own tool allowlist and optionally its own model, and Claude delegates to one automatically when a task matches its description. They can run in parallel and with different permission scopes, for example a read-only researcher alongside a full-access implementer.

nodes.title: Human request
nodes.summary:
Goldy is a set of claude skills and agenst that helps developer keep in touch with what is being done to their codebase when using claude code. 
Goldy does the following:
1. Exports the whole thinking and implementation process for it to be human readable, make an html with nodes  where each node is either a logic decicion node or an action node.
2. Explain your rationale behind every action and decision and in a dropdown, link all the materials and articles that the user needs to know to understand the decision that was done. If you can't fetch the source yourlsef, make a nested document explaining it. Naturally use all the web access you have
3. Format it in a notion like html document with chips, nice animations and colors
nodes.transition: To turn that brief into a plan, the first move was to see what already existed.

nodes.rationale: Before designing anything, ground the build in reality: the actual on-disk layout of skills and agents and the conventions a real installed skill (sentry-cli) follows. Matching existing conventions beats inventing a format Claude Code will not load.

materials.title: Extend Claude with Skills, Claude Code Docs
materials.note: The SKILL.md frontmatter and progressive-disclosure model Goldy's skill is built on.
materials.summary: A skill is a folder containing a SKILL.md file whose YAML frontmatter (name and description) is preloaded into the system prompt so Claude knows when to use it. The fuller instructions, plus optional scripts, references and assets, load only when the skill is actually triggered. This progressive disclosure keeps base context small while still giving the agent deep, on-demand capability.

nodes.title: Survey the project's starting point
nodes.summary: I'll start by understanding the current state of the `goldy` directory and how Claude Code skills/agents are structured.
nodes.transition: That meant inspecting the project and where Claude Code keeps its skills and agents.

nodes.rationale: Listing the project plus the skills and agents directories answers two questions at once, is this a greenfield repo and what is the canonical install location, in a single round trip.

name: sentry-cli
version: 0.27.0
description: Guide for using the Sentry CLI to interact with Sentry from the command line. Use when the user asks about viewing issues, events, projects, organizations, making API calls, or authenticating with Sentry via CLI.
requires:
  bins: ["sentry"]
  auth: true
---

# Sentry CLI Usage Guide

Help users interact with Sentry from the command line using the `sentry` CLI.

## Agent Guidance

Best practices and operational guidance for AI coding agents using the Sentry CLI.

### Key Principles

- **Just
…[truncated 2652 chars]
nodes.transition: Reading an installed skill revealed a pattern worth naming.

nodes.title: Progressive disclosure: load only what is needed, when needed
nodes.summary: A skill exposes just its name and description up front; the full instructions and scripts load only when it is actually used.
nodes.rationale: Goldy follows the same idea internally. The report shows a one-line title and chips first, and tucks the command, result and references into drawers. The reader controls how much detail to reveal instead of being shown everything at once.

materials.title: Progressive Disclosure (Nielsen Norman Group)
materials.note: The interaction-design principle the drawers and the skill's metadata-first loading both apply.

materials.title: Equipping agents for the real world with Agent Skills, Anthropic
materials.note: Anthropic's rationale for the skills architecture: why bundled scripts plus instructions beat a megaprompt.
materials.summary: Anthropic frames skills as a way to package real-world procedures as instructions plus bundled code and assets, instead of stuffing everything into a single prompt. Only a skill's short description stays in context until it is invoked, so an agent can carry many capabilities without bloating its system prompt. The result composes better and is easier to maintain than one giant instruction blob.

nodes.transition: With the skill format understood, the question became where the report's content comes from.

nodes.rationale: Key realization: the whole thinking and implementation process is not something to reconstruct, Claude Code already persists it as a session transcript. Sourcing from the transcript makes Goldy faithful rather than a paraphrase.

materials.title: Claude Code session transcripts (JSONL logs)
materials.doc:
## Where the trace lives

Claude Code writes every session to a JSON-Lines file under:

`~/.claude/projects/<slug>/<session-id>.jsonl`

The **slug** is the project's absolute path with `/` and `.` replaced by `-`. Each line is one event object with a `type`:

- `user` and `assistant`: message turns; `message.content` is an array of blocks (`text`, `thinking`, `tool_use`, `tool_result`).
- `file-history-snapshot`, `mode`, `permission-mode`: session bookkeeping.

Because it is append-only JSONL, Goldy can parse it line by line without loading the whole session into memory, and a crashed write only loses the last line.

materials.title: JSON Lines (JSONL) format
materials.note: The append-friendly, line-delimited JSON format Claude Code uses for transcripts.

nodes.title: Source the report from the session transcript
nodes.summary: The directory is empty — a fresh project. Goldy needs to capture Claude Code's thinking/actions, which live in the session transcript JSONL files. Let me inspect that format so I build against the real data shape.

alternatives.tradeoff: Wrap Claude Code behind a proxy that records traffic live. Captures everything, but needs a running service and changes how users launch the tool.
Read what was printed to the screen. No extra files, but fragile and it loses the thinking and tool metadata that the structured log keeps.
Cheap and simple, but it is a paraphrase rather than the real trace and can drift from what actually happened.

nodes.transition: The answer was the session transcript, so its on-disk format had to be opened up.

nodes.rationale: Do not trust assumptions about the log schema, inspect it. Dumping the first events' types and keys confirms where messages, modes and snapshots live before writing a parser against them.
nodes.title: Inspect session transcript JSONL format

---sample keys---

permission-mode | keys: ['type', 'permissionMode', 'sessionId']
file-history-snapshot | keys: ['type', 'messageId', 'snapshot', 'isSnapshotUpdate']
nodes.transition: Seeing the raw events, the next step was to measure how often each kind occurs.

nodes.rationale: Counting event types and content-block types maps the whole transcript's shape at a glance, confirming that reasoning (thinking and text) and actions (tool_use and tool_result) are cleanly separable into the two node kinds Goldy needs.

materials.title: Anthropic Messages API, tool use
materials.note: Explains the tool_use and tool_result pairing the parser relies on to match an action to its result.

nodes.title: Analyze message and content block types
nodes.transition: Those events also reach out to the web, which is worth a short detour.

nodes.title: Networking: how this report reaches the web
nodes.summary:
Goldy is itself a networked tool. A few concepts make its links precise and durable:

- **URL anatomy**: every reference is a URL with a scheme (https), a host (docs.python.org), a path (/3/library/os.path.html), an optional query string (explainshell uses ?cmd=...) and a fragment (#os.path.expanduser, which jumps to the exact definition).
- **HTTP requests**: the research phase makes HTTP GET requests through WebFetch and WebSearch. When a site answered 403 Forbidden (a client-error status code), Goldy fell back to indexing it from search instead.
- **API versioning**: doc links pin a version (docs.python.org/3/, Godot's /en/stable/) so they keep resolving to the right reference as upstream evolves.
- **HTTPS and TLS**: links use https, so the connection to each source is encrypted and the server's identity is verified.
nodes.rationale: Reading a URL's parts, knowing what a status code means, and pinning an API version are what let Goldy deep-link to an exact, versioned, encrypted source rather than a fragile guess. The same literacy helps a developer debug any request, in any tool.

materials.title: What is a URL (MDN)
materials.note: Read the 'Anatomy of a URL' section: scheme, host, port, path, query, fragment.
materials.summary: A URL is made of a scheme (https), an authority (host and optional port), a path, an optional query string after ?, and an optional fragment after #. Each part has a distinct job: the query passes parameters and the fragment points at a location within the resource. This is exactly what lets a link target one function on a docs page.

materials.title: HTTP response status codes (MDN)
materials.note: The 1xx to 5xx classes; 403 Forbidden is why LabEx was indexed from search.
materials.summary: HTTP status codes are grouped: 2xx success, 3xx redirection, 4xx client errors, 5xx server errors. A 403 means the server understood the request but refuses to authorize it, so a crawler must adapt rather than retry blindly.

materials.title: Versioning a RESTful web API (Microsoft)
materials.note: URI, query-string, header and media-type versioning compared.
materials.summary: An API can be versioned in the URI path (/v3/), in a query string, in a custom header, or via media types. Each trades off visibility, caching and client simplicity. Pinning the version in the path, as the Python and Godot docs do, makes a link stable and obvious.

materials.title: HTTP (MDN)
materials.note: The full reference for requests, responses, methods, headers and caching.

nodes.transition: Back in the transcript, one more check settled how a single turn is ordered.

nodes.rationale: A final shape check of the actual thinking, text, tool_use ordering inside an assistant turn. This confirmed the reason-then-act grouping the parser uses to merge narration into one decision node ahead of its actions.
nodes.title: Show assistant thinking/text/tool_use sequence
nodes.result_excerpt:
THINK:

nodes.transition: With the shape fully confirmed, the data model could be locked in.

nodes.rationale: With the schema verified, the design locks in: decision nodes from thinking and text, action nodes from tool_use paired with their result. Verifying before building avoids a parser written against an imagined format.

materials.title: Equipping agents for the real world with Agent Skills, Anthropic
materials.note: Anthropic's rationale for the skills architecture: why bundled scripts plus instructions beat a megaprompt.
materials.summary: Anthropic frames skills as a way to package real-world procedures as instructions plus bundled code and assets, instead of stuffing everything into a single prompt. Only a skill's short description stays in context until it is invoked, so an agent can carry many capabilities without bloating its system prompt. The result composes better and is easier to maintain than one giant instruction blob.

nodes.title: Lock in the parse model: decisions and actions
nodes.summary:
I now understand the transcript shape: assistant messages carry `thinking`/`text`/`tool_use` blocks, and `tool_result`s come back in user messages. That's everything Goldy needs. Let me build the project.

I'll create the canonical repo in `goldy/` (parser + renderer + skill + agent) and install it into `~/.claude`.
nodes.transition: That decision rests on a principle worth stating plainly.

nodes.title: Separation of concerns: parse, enrich, render
nodes.summary: Three stages with one job each, talking only through nodes.json.
nodes.rationale: Each stage can be tested and replaced on its own: swap the renderer's theme without touching the parser, re-enrich without re-parsing. Coupling them into one script would make every change risky.

materials.title: Separation of concerns
materials.note: The foundational idea behind the three-stage split.
materials.summary: Separation of concerns means dividing a program so each part addresses one concern, with minimal overlap. When concerns are isolated behind clear interfaces, you can understand, change or replace one part without disturbing the others. It is the general principle that justifies splitting parsing, enrichment and rendering into independent stages.

materials.title: The Clean Architecture (Robert C. Martin)
materials.note: Read the section 'The Dependency Rule': source dependencies may only point inward, which keeps outer details (UI, DB) swappable.
materials.summary: The post collects several architectures (hexagonal, onion, others) into one model of concentric layers, with business rules at the center and frameworks, UI and databases at the edges. Its core is the Dependency Rule: source-code dependencies may only point inward, so inner layers know nothing about outer ones. That is what lets you swap a detail like the renderer or the database without touching the policy at the core.

nodes.transition: It pairs with a second habit visible all through the build.

nodes.title: Preparation phase: learn first, then write
nodes.summary: Every writing step in this build was preceded by a learning step. The directory was inspected, the transcript schema was dumped and verified, and only then was code written against the real shape of the data.
nodes.rationale: Understanding the ground truth before changing anything prevents whole classes of bug: the parser was written against a schema that had been confirmed by hand, not imagined. The cost of a few read-only checks up front is far smaller than rewriting code built on a wrong assumption.

materials.title: Chesterton's Fence
materials.note: Do not change (or build on) something until you understand why it is the way it is.
materials.summary: Chesterton's Fence is the rule that you should not remove or rely on a thing until you understand why it exists. Applied to engineering, it means learn the existing system and its constraints before you act, because the reason for the current state is often not obvious. It is the principle behind inspecting the transcript format before writing a parser for it.

materials.title: Effective context engineering for AI agents (Anthropic)
materials.note: Gather the right context first; an agent that acts before it understands its inputs makes avoidable mistakes.

materials.title: Measure twice, cut once
materials.note: The craft idiom for verifying before you commit to an irreversible step.

nodes.transition: Grounded by that preparation, the first real change to the repo could happen.

nodes.rationale: Lay the project out the way Claude Code expects: skills/goldy and agents mirror the loader's search paths, and git-init it since this is a GitHub project, so an install script can later symlink it into ~/.claude.

materials.title: Create custom subagents, Claude Code Docs
materials.note: Defines the ~/.claude/agents/*.md format and tool and model scoping used by goldy-historian.
materials.summary: Custom subagents are Markdown files with YAML frontmatter stored in .claude/agents (project) or ~/.claude/agents (user). Each subagent gets its own system prompt, its own tool allowlist and optionally its own model, and Claude delegates to one automatically when a task matches its description. They can run in parallel and with different permission scopes, for example a read-only researcher alongside a full-access implementer.

materials.title: Learn Git with Hands-on Labs (LabEx)
materials.note: Hands-on Git: repositories, staging, branching, merging, collaboration.
materials.summary: Interactive, no-setup Git labs in a real environment, progressing from repositories and branching to team collaboration workflows. The preferred place to practice the git commands this step uses.

materials.title: Shell Scripting Learning Path (LabEx)
materials.note: Hands-on shell: pipes, redirection, variables, scripting.
materials.summary: A systematic, hands-on path for the shell, covering pipes, redirection, variables and scripting through interactive exercises rather than video.

nodes.title: Scaffold goldy repo directories
nodes.detail: mkdir -p skills/goldy/scripts agents .goldy && git init -q 2>/dev/null; echo done
nodes.transition: Creating files and an installer immediately raises a safety concern.

nodes.title: Least privilege and symlink safety in the installer
nodes.summary: The installer only creates symlinks, and never overwrites a real file. Its link() helper skips any destination that already exists and is not a symlink, printing a warning instead of replacing it; otherwise it runs `ln -sfn`, which replaces an existing link in place rather than dereferencing it into a directory.
nodes.rationale: Skipping an existing non-symlink preserves whatever file the user already has at that path, and the -n (no-dereference) flag prevents writing through a symlink into an unintended directory. The installer's footprint is small by design: it creates only ~/.claude/skills and ~/.claude/agents, symlinks the skills and agent, and adds one SessionStart hook entry to settings.json. Goldy reads the session transcripts under ~/.claude read-only and changes no file permissions.

materials.title: CWE-59: Improper Link Resolution Before File Access (Link Following)
materials.note: Why an installer must guard against replacing or following a symlink at a write target.

materials.title: Principle of least privilege
materials.note: The access-control principle behind the installer's minimal, symlink-only footprint.

nodes.transition: With the repo laid out safely, the first script could be designed.

nodes.rationale: Python was chosen for the parser: it ships on macOS with zero install, has first-class JSON, and keeps the mechanical work (transcript to nodes) out of the model so it is deterministic and cheap. The model is reserved for the judgement-heavy enrichment.

materials.title: Python json, encoder and decoder
materials.note: The standard-library module that makes line-by-line JSONL parsing a few lines of code.

materials.title: Learn Python with Hands-on Labs (LabEx)
materials.note: Hands-on Python: syntax, data structures, OOP, file IO.
materials.summary: A structured, interactive Python roadmap covering syntax, data structures and object-oriented programming in a real browser environment. The preferred place to practice the Python this parser is written in.

materials.title: Working with JSON Data in Python (Real Python)
materials.note: A hands-on walkthrough of reading and writing JSON in Python, the exact task the parser does.

nodes.title: Build the transcript parser in Python
nodes.summary: Now the transcript parser — mechanical extraction of decision/action nodes from the JSONL:

alternatives.tradeoff: Equally good at JSON, but it adds a runtime that is not guaranteed on every machine the way Python 3 is on macOS.
Excellent for quick one-line filters, but painful once you need the branching request/decision/action node model.
Most flexible, but nondeterministic, slower, and spends tokens on purely mechanical work.

nodes.transition: How that script reads the file is a performance choice worth noting.

nodes.title: Stream the transcript line by line, not all at once
nodes.summary: The parser reads the JSONL file one line per iteration instead of loading and parsing the whole session into memory.
nodes.rationale: A long session can be large. Iterating the file object yields one line at a time, so memory stays roughly constant and the work is a single O(n) pass. Tool results are indexed once into a dict for O(1) pairing with their tool calls.

materials.title: Reading files lazily in Python
materials.note: Why iterating a file object streams it rather than buffering the entire file.
materials.summary: Iterators produce values one at a time and only when asked, so they let you process data that is large or even unbounded without holding it all in memory. A Python file object is itself an iterator over lines, so looping directly over it reads one line per step. That is exactly how the parser walks a transcript without loading the whole session.

materials.title: Time complexity (Big O)
materials.note: Framing the single-pass parse and the O(1) dict lookups.
materials.summary: Big O describes how an algorithm's running time grows with input size. A single pass over n lines is O(n), and a hash-map (dict) lookup is on average O(1) regardless of size. Indexing tool results into a dict once and then pairing them by id keeps the parser linear instead of quadratic.

alternatives.tradeoff: Simplest to write, but JSONL is not one JSON document, and it would hold the entire session in memory at once.

alternatives.option: Read all lines into a list first
alternatives.tradeoff: Convenient random access, but defeats the streaming win on large transcripts.

nodes.transition: That efficiency reflects a broader stance about where work belongs.

nodes.title: Deterministic core, model at the edges
nodes.summary: Mechanical work (parsing, rendering) is plain Python; judgement (rationale, research) is left to the model.
nodes.rationale: Keeping the model out of the predictable parts makes Goldy cheap, repeatable and easy to debug, and spends model effort only where taste and reasoning actually matter.

materials.title: Building Effective AI Agents (Anthropic)
materials.note: Read the section 'When (and when not) to use agents': find the simplest solution first and add agentic complexity only when it earns its keep.
materials.summary: Anthropic distinguishes workflows (LLMs and tools orchestrated through predefined code paths) from agents (the model dynamically directs its own steps). Its central advice is to find the simplest solution that works and only add complexity, and cost and latency, when the task genuinely needs it. Goldy applies this by keeping deterministic code paths for parsing and rendering and spending model effort only on judgement.

materials.title: The Unix Philosophy
materials.note: See McIlroy's summary: make each program do one thing well and expect its output to become another program's input.
materials.summary: The Unix philosophy favors small programs that each do one thing well and combine through simple, text-based interfaces. McIlroy's famous formulation is to write programs that do one thing well and that work together, expecting the output of one to be the input of another. This is the lineage behind Goldy's parse-to-nodes-to-render pipeline.

nodes.transition: With the approach settled, the parser was actually written.

nodes.rationale: With the JSONL schema already confirmed by hand (the learning step just before), the parser could be written directly against the real shape. It is intentionally minimal: it extracts raw facts and leaves rationale and materials empty, so extraction stays separate from interpretation and each piece is independently testable.

materials.title: Python: dictionaries
materials.note: The dict used to index tool results by id for O(1) pairing with their tool calls.

materials.title: Working with JSON Data in Python (Real Python)
materials.note: A hands-on guide to the json parsing the script does line by line.

materials.title: Separation of concerns
materials.note: Why parsing, enrichment and rendering are three swappable stages rather than one script.

nodes.title: Write parse_transcript.py
nodes.result_excerpt: File created successfully at: /Users/vbatocanin/Dev/GitHub/goldy/skills/goldy/scripts/parse_transcript.py (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)
nodes.transition: Writing it surfaced a question about how much output to keep.

nodes.title: Cap captured output to bound size and cost
nodes.summary: Each result excerpt and command body is truncated to a fixed budget before it ever reaches nodes.json.
nodes.rationale: Capping output keeps nodes.json small, keeps the rendered HTML light in the browser, and crucially bounds the token cost of the enrichment step, where the model reads every node. The cap is explicit so the tradeoff between detail and size is visible in one place.

materials.title: Why payload size matters for web performance
materials.note: Background on keeping rendered document weight down.

materials.title: Token-based pricing and context limits
materials.note: Why bounding the text the model reads during enrichment controls cost.

nodes.transition: Capping output also helps keep something dangerous out of the report.

nodes.title: Secret hygiene: keep tokens and keys out of the report
nodes.summary: A session trace can contain API keys, tokens or private paths from command output. The report must not leak them.
nodes.rationale: The parser caps every captured result, and the skill scans enriched text for secrets before rendering. A Goldy report is meant to be shared (committed, emailed), so treating the output as untrusted and redacting is the safe default.

materials.title: OWASP: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
materials.note: Practices for handling secrets so they do not end up in logs or artifacts.

materials.title: CWE-532: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File
materials.note: The exact weakness a trace tool risks: leaking secrets through captured output.
materials.summary: CWE-532 is the weakness where an application writes sensitive data (credentials, tokens, personal data) into logs or trace files that are less protected than the data deserves. The fix is to keep secrets out of captured output and to scrub or redact before anything is persisted or shared. A session-trace tool like Goldy is squarely in this risk category, which is why it caps and scans output before rendering.

nodes.transition: With extraction safe and bounded, the final stage could be designed.

nodes.rationale: The renderer emits a single self-contained HTML file (inline CSS and JS, no network). That makes a report portable, emailable, committable, viewable offline, and the Notion-like styling (spine graph, chips, drawers, scroll-in animation) is pure CSS so it never breaks.

materials.title: HTML details and summary disclosure element
materials.note: The native, JS-free primitive powering Goldy's Learn more and code drawers.

materials.title: CSS keyframe animations
materials.note: How the staggered node rise-in effect is done with delays and no JavaScript.

materials.title: Using CSS animations, step by step (CSS-Tricks)
materials.note: A practical tutorial for building the kind of keyframe effects the report uses.

nodes.title: Render one self-contained HTML report
nodes.summary: Now the renderer — a standalone, dependency-free Notion-style HTML document with the node graph, chips, animations, and material dropdowns:

alternatives.tradeoff: Tools like Eleventy produce polished output, but add a build step and dependencies to install and keep current.
Very portable, but static: no hover tips and no drawers, so the interactive learning parts are lost.
Rich and componentized, but needs bundling and a server, and will rot as its dependencies age.

nodes.transition: That design then had to become real code.

nodes.rationale: render.py keeps a tiny safe markdown converter and the full template in one file so a report can be regenerated from nodes.json alone, no build step, no dependencies, easy to audit before sharing.

materials.title: Python: regular expressions (re)
materials.note: The re module behind the tiny markdown converter and the shell and Python tokenizers.

materials.title: Python string formatting (f-strings)
materials.note: How the HTML is assembled from node data without a templating dependency.

materials.title: Notion-style document design
materials.note: The clean-card, chip and toggle vocabulary the report's visual language borrows from.

nodes.title: Write render.py
nodes.detail:
# /Users/vbatocanin/Dev/GitHub/goldy/skills/goldy/scripts/render.py #!/usr/bin/env python3 """Goldy — renderer. Turns an (enriched) nodes JSON into a single self-contained, Notion-styled HTML file: a vertical graph of decision/action nodes, each with a rationale and a collapsible "Learn more" drawer that links every supporting article or, when no URL is available, renders a nested explainer document inline. Usage: python3 render.py nodes.json -o goldy-report.html """ import argparse import html import json import re import sys from datetime import datetime # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # tiny, safe markdown -> html (paragraphs, code, bold/italic, links, lists) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- def md(text): if not text: return "" text = html.escape(text) # fenced code def fence(m): return f"
{m.group(1)}
" text = re.sub(r"```[a-zA-Z0-9]*\n(.*?)```", fence, text, flags=re.S) # inline code text = re.sub(r"`([^`]+)`", r"\1", text) # links [t](u) text = re.sub(r"\[([^\]]+)\]\((https?://[^)]+)\)", r"\1", text) # bold / italic text = re.sub(r"\*\*([^*]+)\*\*", r"\1", text) text = re.sub(r"(?\1", text) # paragraphs / line breaks blocks = [] for para in re.split(r"\n\s*\n", text.strip()): if para.startswith("{re.sub(r'^\s*[-*] ', '', ln)}" for ln in para.splitlines() if ln.strip()) blocks.append(f"
    {items}
") else: blocks.append("

" + para.replace("\n", "
") + "

") return "\n".join(blocks) KIND = { "prompt": {"label": "Request", "icon": "✦", "cls": "k-prompt"}, "decision": {"label": "Decision", "icon": "◆", "cls": "k-decision"}, "action": {"label": "Action", "icon": "▸", "cls": "k-action"}, } TOOL_ICON = { "Bash": "⌘", "Edit": "✎", "Write": "✚", "Read": "▤", "Grep": "⌕", "Glob": "⌕", "WebFetch": "🌐", "WebSearch": "🔎", "Agent": "🤖", "Task": "🤖", } def chip(text, cls=""): return f"{html.escape(text)}" def materials_html(materials): if not materials: return ("
No external references attached to this " "step.
") out = [] for m in materials: title = html.escape(m.get("title", "Reference")) note = md(m.get("note", "")) url = m.get("url") doc = m.get("doc") if url: host = re.sub(r"^https?://(www\.)?", "", url).split("/")[0] out.append( f"🔗" f"{title}" f"{html.escape(host)}" f"{f'{note}' if note else ''}") elif doc: out.append( "
📄" f"{title}" "nested explainer" f"
{md(doc)}
") else: out.append(f"
•" f"{title}" f"{note}
") return "\n".join(out) def node_html(n, idx): k = KIND.get(n["kind"], KIND["decision"]) chips = [chip(k["label"], k["cls"])] if n.get("tool"): …[truncated 10230 chars]
nodes.result_excerpt: File created successfully at: /Users/vbatocanin/Dev/GitHub/goldy/skills/goldy/scripts/render.py (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)
nodes.transition: Once written, the renderer had to be verified.

nodes.title: How to verify a change like this
nodes.summary:
The renderer was checked at several levels, cheapest first:

- **Unit**: call the pure token functions (annotate_bash_inner, annotate_python_inner) directly and assert the token classes, for example that '2>' is a redirection and '/dev/null' is a device file.
- **Integration**: run the whole parse, enrich, render pipeline and grep the HTML for expected structure (chip counts, drawer markers, SVG icon count, zero em dashes).
- **UI**: open the report in a headless browser, screenshot it, and read it back to confirm layout, colours and the hover affordances actually render.
- **Manual**: open .goldy/goldy-report.html and click the What ran and Result toggles and hover tokens.
nodes.rationale: Match the test to the risk. Pure functions get fast unit checks; the end-to-end shape gets a cheap integration grep; anything visual (a Notion-like report) needs a real render and a look, because HTML and CSS bugs never show up in asserts.

materials.title: The Practical Test Pyramid (Martin Fowler)
materials.note: Read the 'The Test Pyramid' section: many fast unit tests, fewer integration, fewest slow end-to-end.
materials.summary: The test pyramid recommends writing many small, fast unit tests, fewer integration tests, and only a few slow end-to-end tests. Higher-level tests are more realistic but slower and more brittle, so push coverage down to the cheapest level that still catches the bug. It is the rationale for unit-testing the token functions and only screenshotting the whole report a few times.

materials.title: Python unittest
materials.note: The standard-library framework for the unit-level checks on the pure functions.

materials.title: Playwright for Python
materials.note: Driving a real browser to render, screenshot and assert on the UI.

nodes.transition: The way it was verified points to one last principle.

nodes.title: Single file, zero dependencies, portable output
nodes.summary: The report is one HTML file with inline CSS and JS and no network needs.
nodes.rationale: A document you can email, commit or open offline in five years outlives any toolchain. No build step and no package install means nothing to rot, and the whole artifact is auditable before it is shared.

materials.title: The Art of Unix Programming (Eric S. Raymond)
materials.note: On simplicity, transparency and durable, text-first artifacts.

materials.title: Data URLs and self-contained documents (MDN)
materials.note: Background on bundling assets inline so a file stands alone.

nodes.transition: Taken together, those choices add up to one closing picture.

nodes.title: In summary: the path from session to report
nodes.summary:
Goldy turns a raw Claude Code session into a portable, Notion-style walkthrough, and the build followed one consistent spine:

- **Source of truth**: read the real session transcript instead of paraphrasing it.
- **Three stages**: parse extracts the facts, enrich adds the reasoning and sources, render emits the page, talking only through nodes.json.
- **Deterministic core**: the mechanical work is plain Python; the model is spent only on judgement.
- **Safe by default**: captured output is capped and scanned so secrets never reach a shared report.
- **One durable file**: the result is a single dependency-free HTML document you can email, commit or open offline.
nodes.rationale: Read top to bottom, the steps above are that story in detail: learn before writing, separate the stages, keep the predictable parts predictable, and leave behind an artifact that outlives its toolchain.

materials.title: Extend Claude with Skills, Claude Code Docs
materials.note: The SKILL.md frontmatter and progressive-disclosure model Goldy's skill is built on.
materials.summary: A skill is a folder containing a SKILL.md file whose YAML frontmatter (name and description) is preloaded into the system prompt so Claude knows when to use it. The fuller instructions, plus optional scripts, references and assets, load only when the skill is actually triggered. This progressive disclosure keeps base context small while still giving the agent deep, on-demand capability.

materials.title: Notion-style document design
materials.note: The clean-card visual language the finished report borrows from.
